coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal elements were widely used in alloy manufacturing, medical treatment, food additive, military industry and other fields, which were harmful to human body. Optical detection was a non-contact nondestructive detection method, which had the advantages of high selectivity, high sensitivity and low cost, and had important applications and prospects in environmental monitoring and biological science. In this paper, the optical detection methods, detection principles and detection methods of 12 kinds of metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+,Hg2+,Pb2+, Pd2+,Zn2+) were reviewed. Fluorescence, colorimetry and spectral change were the main detection methods. Fluorescence detection methods mainly included fluorescence quenching, fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence spectrum changes; The colorimetric method included the change of color and the change of ultraviolet absorption spectrum; The spectral changes mainly included circular dichroism, phosphorescence and surface enhanced Raman scattering, etc. Photoinduced electron transfer, intramolecular charge transfer and fluorescence resonance energy transfer were the primary principles of optical detection. The application prospect and main challenges of optical detection were discussed.

head.ce:title: Methods, principles and applications of optical detection of metal ios

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal elements were widely used in alloy manufacturing, medical treatment, food additive, military industry and other fields, which were harmful to human body. Optical detection was a non-contact nondestructive detection method, which had the advantages of high selectivity, high sensitivity and low cost, and had important applications and prospects in environmental monitoring and biological science. In this paper, the optical detection methods, detection principles and detection methods of 12 kinds of metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+,Hg2+,Pb2+, Pd2+,Zn2+) were reviewed. Fluorescence, colorimetry and spectral change were the main detection methods. Fluorescence detection methods mainly included fluorescence quenching, fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence spectrum changes; The colorimetric method included the change of color and the change of ultraviolet absorption spectrum; The spectral changes mainly included circular dichroism, phosphorescence and surface enhanced Raman scattering, etc. Photoinduced electron transfer, intramolecular charge transfer and fluorescence resonance energy transfer were the primary principles of optical detection. The application prospect and main challenges of optical detection were discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of industrial production and manufacturing industry, the pollution of heavy metal ions is more and more serious. The waste liquid containing heavy metal ions, which is discharged into the environment, pollutes the soil and water sources, and then affects crops, animals and plants, and eventually accumulates in the human body, resulting in serious diseases [1–4]. For example, the toxicity of aluminum ions is not only harmful to some animals and crops [5,6], but also causes severe damage to the central nervous system of the human body, leading to serious diseases such as encephalopathy and Alzheimer's disease [7,8]. Copper ions, which are widely used in industry, can also cause neurological diseases such as Parkinson's disease and Wilson's disease in the human body [9,10]. Mercury ions, for example, are highly toxic and do extremely serious harm to human health and the environment [11,12]. Therefore, sensitive and efficient detection of heavy metal ions in food, clinical medicine and environment is particularly important.

In order to detect heavy metal ions, over the past few decades, a variety of detection methods came into being. Some detection techniques, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [13], electrochemical sensor [14], X-ray fluorescence [15], optical way [16] and so on, had made remarkable progress and achievements. Among them, compared with other methods, the optical method had the unique advantages of high selectivity, high sensitivity, low cost and simple operation mode [17]. Optical sensors for the detection of various heavy metal ions had been developed rapidly. The optical methods for detecting metal ions mainly fall into the following categories: (1) Fluorescence method [18,19]. The metal ions are selectively detected by fluorescence enhancement, quenching or variation in fluorescence spectrum. (2) Colorimetry [20,21]. The metal ions are selectively detected by colorimetric change or changes in the ultraviolet absorption spectrum. (3) Other methods [22,23]. Including surface enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy and circular dichroism spectroscopy, all of them can be used for selective detection of metal ions.

In this review, 12 common optical detection methods of metal ions, including their principles and phenomena, were reviewed (metal ions: Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+,Hg2+,Pb2+, Pd2+,Zn2+). Firstly, the main optical detection principles of PET (photo-induced electron transfer), ICT (intramolecular charge transfer), FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) and so on were introduced. Then, the representative detection methods of each metal ion were described systematically, including the detection principle and experimental phenomena. Finally, the application prospect and main challenges of optical detection were discussed.

The main optical detection methods for heavy metal ions are fluorescence detection and colorimetric detection. In addition, the changes of fluorescence emission spectrum or ultraviolet absorption spectrum, phosphorescence quenching and circular dichroism spectrum are also effective methods for optical detection of heavy metal ions. Based on the principles of photoinduced electron transfer, intramolecular charge transfer and fluorescence resonance energy transfer, many new metal ion sensors with high selectivity had been designed and great progress had been made too.

In the fluorescence detection of various metal ions, the fluorescence sensors based on the principle of photo-induced electron transfer (PET) are the most common [24–26]. When excited by light, the electron transfer occurred between the electron donor and the electron acceptor, resulting in the fluorescence weakening or even quenching of the fluorophore. After the analyte was combined with the sensor, the PET effect was suppressed and the fluorescence emission of the fluorophore was restored [27]. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), when the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the receptor was between the energy of the two orbitals of the fluorescent group, the receptor with the ability of electron donation can transfer the electrons in the HOMO into the HOMO, vacated by the fluorescent group due to electron excitation, so that the electrons excited by light cannot directly jump back to the original ground state orbital to emit fluorescence, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the fluorescent group. When the lowest unoccupied molecule orbital (LUMO) of the receptor was between the energy of the two orbitals of the fluorophore, after the fluorescent group was excited by light, one of the electrons in the HOMO jumped to the LUMO, and then transfered to the empty LUMO of the receptor, and finally returned to the HOMO of the fluorophore. The excited electrons returned to the ground state in a non-radiative way, resulting in fluorescence quenching. A typical PET sensing system was consisted of a receptor containing an electron donor, which was connected to the fluorophore through the spacer group. Under the excitation of light, the process of PET between the receptor and the fluorophore occurred, which quenched the fluorescence. When the receptor bound to the analyte, the PET process between the receptor and the fluorophore was inhibited, thus restoring the fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Sensors based on the principle of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) can detect metal ions efficiently and sensitively [28,29].

A typical fluorescence sensor based on the principle of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) had both an electron-pushing group (electron donor) and an electron-absorbing group (electron acceptor) connected to the fluorescent group. Under the excitation of light, the charge of the electron donor transfered to the electron acceptor, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) [30,31]. And the electron-pushing group or the electron-absorbing group itself contained the recognition group. When detecting metal cations (electron-deficient groups), there were two situations: one was the combination of metal ions with electron-pushing groups, so that the arc-pair electrons originally transferred to the conjugated system were used to form coordination bonds with cations, which weakened the intramolecular charge transfer, resulting in the blue shift of fluorescence spectrum; The other was the combination of metal ions with electron-absorbing groups, which enhanced the intramolecular charge transfer and led to the red shift of fluorescence spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2(b). Sensors based on the mechanism of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) mechanism had been widely used in the detection of metal ions [32,33].

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer system (FRET) meant that a fluorescence system contained two different fluorescent groups, one as an energy donor (D), and the other as an energy acceptor (A). When the excitation wavelength of donor D was used to excite the fluorescence system, the non-radiative energy transfer from D to A might occur [34,35]. If the fluorescence quantum yield of receptor A was zero, the fluorescence of the system was extinguished after energy transfer, and if the receptor was also a fluorescence emitter, it showed the fluorescence of the receptor. The generation of FRET needed certain conditions. There were mainly: (1) the distance between the energy donor and the acceptor must be close enough; (2) there was a certain overlap between the emission spectrum of the energy donor and the absorption spectrum of the energy acceptor; (3) the donor and the acceptor must be arranged in an appropriate way. As shown in Fig. 3 , only if these conditions were met will an effective FRET be achieved. The fluorescence sensor based on FRET bound to the analyte by bonding groups, thus changing the distance between D and A, arrangement and other factors. If the addition of analyte kept these factors in the appropriate range, energy donor D would transfer energy to energy receptor A by non-radiative means, and receptor A would show specific fluorescence changes; If the addition of analyte did not make these factors meet the requirements of FRET occurrence, then FRET would be inhibited, with the fluorescence emission of D, as shown in Fig. 4
[36–38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection method based on nanoparticles/quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Some nanoparticles (NPs) and their functional states (such as silver nanoparticles, gold nanoparticles, etc) had attracted much attention because of their unique electronic, photonic and catalytic properties [39,40]. And it had been used in many important applications in the detection of metal ions, as fluorescent and colorimetric sensors to detect metal ions. Similarly, some quantum dots and their functional states (such as carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, etc.) had the characteristics of good stability, good solubility, good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, so they were widely used in the detection of metal ions [41–43]. There are three main principles of detection: (1) The binding or reaction of nanoparticles or quantum dots with metal ions, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). For example, specific metal bonds were formed between metal nanoparticles and metal ions, which generally changed the optical properties of the whole system. Redox reactions of metal ions might also occur; (2) The binding or reaction of ligands or modifiers on the surface of nanoparticles/quantum dots with metal ions, as shown in Fig. 5(b); and (3) Metal ions induced the aggregation of nanoparticles or quantum dots, as shown in Fig. 5(c) [44–47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection method based on rhodamine and its derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: The detection principle of metal ion sensors based on rhodamine and its derivatives was based on the fluorescence characteristics of rhodamine. This property of Rhodamine was related to the open loop and closed loop of the five-membrance in its structure [48]. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the left picture showed the open-loop rhodamine structure, showing strong fluorescence; the right picture showed the closed-loop rhodamine structure, with the fluorescence disappearing. The open-loop and closed-loop states of rhodamine can be converted to each other under certain conditions. Metal ions induced the transformation of the spirolactam structure of rhodamine into ring-opening structure (ring-opening), resulting in different fluorescent and colorimetric changes. Different rhodamine derivatives were designed and synthesized in different ways, which showed uniquely fluorescent changes or colorimetric response in the presence of different metal ions, so as to achieve the purpose of detecting metal ions, as shown in Fig. 6(b) [49–51].

Silver ions had many applications in the electrical, photographic and medical industries, which resulted in large amounts of silver ions being released into the environment each year. Excessive silver ions will not only cause cell poisoning and inactivation of sulfhydryl enzymes and other effects on human body, but also caused pollution to the environment. In order to detect silver ions, Kumar designed and prepared a novel Ag+ fluorescence chemical sensor [52] based on thio-calixarene, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). And the detection principle was that the binding of silver ion and thio-calixarene receptor will change the receptor from the original excimer emission to monomer emission, so that the fluorescence emission spectrum was quenched at 470 nm and enhanced at 377 nm, as shown in Fig. 7(b). Zhang reported a fluorescent sensor based on cytidine-stabilized gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) [53], which can quickly and sensitively identify Ag+. As shown in Fig. 7(c), the combination of the sensor and Ag+ resulted in a unique fluorescence turn-on phenomenon, which may be related to the formation of Au-Ag metal bond. It can be observed from Fig. 7(d) that the prepared gold nanoclusters emitted weak green fluorescence under ultraviolet (365 nm) irradiation, while AuAg NCs emitted strong yellow fluorescence after the addition of silver ions, and the emission intensity increased monotonously with the increase of Ag+ concentration. Shi designed and synthesized a fluorescence Schiff base derivative (M1) with simple structure containing hydrazine thioamide [54]. The interaction between M1 and Ag+ produced the photo-induced electron-transfer (PET) effect, as shown in Fig. 7(e). By studying the optical response of M1 to common metal ions, it can be seen that silver ion had a unique partial quenching and red-shift effect on the fluorescence spectrum of M1, as shown in Fig. 7(f).

In addition to some compounds that can be used as fluorescent probes to detect silver ions, some quantum dots are also very suitable as fluorescent probes due to their better stability and high biocompatibility. Gao reported a simple method for the preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots [55], as shown in Fig. 8 (a). After the addition of silver ions, the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots significantly increased and showed a linear relationship with the concentration of Ag+, as shown in Fig. 8(b). This was attributed to the reduction of silver ions to Ag clusters on the surface of carbon quantum dots. Bian had prepared and reported a sulfur-doped graphene quantum dot (S-GQD) for Ag+ detection [56], as shown in Fig. 8(c). Due to the synergistic effect of S and O functional groups on S-GQDS, S-GQDS was highly selective to Ag+. It can be seen from Fig. 8(d) that the addition of silver ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of S-GQD. With the increase of Ag+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of S-GQDs decreased gradually. Based on a synthetic amine-terminated graphene quantum dot (Am-GQDs), Suryawanshi developed a fluorescence sensor for silver ion detection [57]. The fluorescence of Am-GQDs was quenched by the addition of Ag+, and then regenerated after the addition of L-cysteine, as shown in Fig. 8(f), and only the combination of Ag+ and L-cysteine could cause the process of fluorescence turn-off and re-opening. The principle was that L-cysteine groups had a strong affinity for Ag+ ions. The addition of L-cysteine can release Ag+ attached to Am-GQD, causing the fluorescence to turn on, as shown in Fig. 8(e).

Besides fluorescence detection, colorimetric method can detect silver ions quickly and intuitively. Chang reported a fast and sensitive Ag+ colorimetric sensor based on the activity of gold nanocluster peroxidase stabilized by bovine serum albumin (BSA @ AuNCs) [58]. BSA@AuNCs has peroxidase activity and can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2. However, after adding Ag+, BSA@AuNCs enzyme activity was significantly inhibited, making the reaction impossible, as shown in Fig. 9 (a). This was due to the strong Ag-Au interaction. Fig. 9(b) showed three scenarios: without BSA@AuNCs, there was no reaction between TMB and H2O2 (curve A); BSA@AuNCs catalyzed the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to produce strong colored products (curve B); BSA@AuNCs could hardly catalyze the chromogenic reaction between TMB and H2O2 (curve C) after adding Ag+. Hung reported a new detection method based on mercaptans and gold nanoparticles, which can detect Ag+ quickly and efficiently [59]. In the 2-mercaptoethanol/Au NPs system, the presence of Ag+ leaded to the significant aggregation of gold nanoparticles, which was attributed to the deposition of silver ions on the surface of gold nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 9(c). At the same time, the addition of Ag+ caused the red shift of the UV–vis absorption spectrum, as shown in Fig. 9(d).

In addition to the fluorescence spectrum and colorimetric changes, the changes of optical absorption spectrum and emission band caused by some substances and metal ions can also be used to detect silver ions. Based on the redshift of the emission wavelength of CdTe Quantum dots (QDs) wrapped in TGA, Wang designed and reported a new method for detecting Ag+ [60], as shown in Fig. 10 (a). It can be seen from Fig. 10(b), as the concentration of silver ions added to CdTe QDs solution increased, the fluorescence emission peak gradually strengthened and moved to a longer wavelength. The illustration showed a good linear relationship between emission wavelength redshift (Y) and silver ion concentration. Li reported a method for the detection of silver ions based on the enzyme activity changes of quadruplex/ duplex DNA structure (G4.ds (C–C)) [61]. After the addition of Ag+, C-Ag+-C base pairs were formed to enhance the DNA activity of G4.ds (C–C), as shown in Fig. 10(c). With the increase of Ag+ concentration, the absorption intensity of the system gradually increased, and the DNA enzyme activity of G4.ds(C–C) also gradually increased, as shown in Fig. 10(d).

As the most abundant metal element in the earth's crust, aluminum is widely used in medicine, automobile manufacturing, food additives, alloy appliances and other fields. This makes aluminum ions easily exposed to the environment, which does great harm to the environment and human health. Excessive aluminum ions in the body can cause a number of serious diseases, such as osteoporosis, encephalopathy and Alzheimer's disease. In order to detect aluminum ions, Jiang designed and synthesized a novel fluorescence sensor based on 8-hydroxyquinoline formaldehyde Schiff base [62]. The combination of the sensor and Al3+ effectively suppressed the PET phenomenon of Schiff base and quinoline nitrogen atoms, thus significantly enhancing the fluorescence intensity of the sensor, as shown in Fig. 11 (a). Fig. 11(b) showed that the fluorescence intensity of the sensor increased with the addition of Al3+. Xu reported a newly synthesized three-dimensional lanthanide-organic framework {[Eu (BTB) (phen)]· 4.5DMF·2H2O}n for the detection and recovery of aluminum ions [63], and its schematic diagram was shown in Fig. 11(c). In the ethanol solution of the framework sample, the luminescence intensity was measured after adding different metal ions. As shown in Fig. 11(d), aluminum ions had an obvious quenching effect on it, which may be attributed to the energy consumption caused by the collision interaction between the framework and free aluminum ions, resulting in the decrease of luminous intensity. Gui reported a novel fluorescence-turned-on chemical sensor 2 (4-(1,2,2-triphenylethyl) phenoxy) acetic acid (TPE-COOH) for the detection of aluminum ions [64]. When Al3+ was added to the solution of the sensor, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced and there was blue fluorescence visible to the naked eye. This was due to the aggregation of TPE-COOH induced by aluminum ions, resulting in significant fluorescence emission, as shown in Fig. 11(e). Fig. 11(f) showed that the fluorescence of 470 nm emission gradually increased with the increase of concentration of Al3+. And the sensor had been successfully applied to the detection of aluminum ions in living cells.

In addition to fluorescence enhancement and quenching, aluminum ions can also be effectively detected by the change of fluorescence spectrum. Zou successfully designed a fluorescence sensor based on fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) [65]. Fig. 12 (a) showed the binding principle of CNDs and Al3+, the mechanism of which was the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between them. With the increase of Al3+ concentration, the fluorescence spectra of carbon nanoparticles changed, and the intensity increased, as shown in Fig. 12(b). Yang reported a rhodamine-benzothiazole fluorescent probe (P) based on the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which can effectively detect aluminum ions [66]. The reaction diagram was shown in Fig. 12(c). The addition of Al3+ caused the energy transfer from benzothiazole to rodanbenzenyl group. As the concentration of Al3+ increased, the fluorescence of the probe increased at 586 nm and decreased at 535 nm, as shown in Fig. 12(d).

In addition to fluorescence detection of Al3+, colorimetry can be used to quickly and intuitively detect aluminum ions. Xue reported a new type of Al3+ colorimetric probe: MMT-AUNP probe was formed by modifying 5-mercaptomethyl tetrazole (MMT) as chelating ligand to the surface of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [67]. The addition of Al3+ induced the aggregation of MMT-AuNPs, resulting in color changes, as shown in Fig. 13 (a). When titrated with different concentrations of aluminum ions, the absorption spectrum of the probe decreased at 520 nm and produced a new absorption peak at 620 nm, as shown in Fig. 13(b), and the color of the solution changed from red to dark blue. Borase reported a new synthetic Schiff base receptor (E)-1-(4-((4-(diethylamino) −2-hydroxybenzylidene) amino) phenyl) −3, 4-diphenyl −1H-pyrrole-2, 5-dione (BASB) which was used as colorimetry sensor for selective detection of aluminum ions [68]. As shown in Fig. 13(c), the color of BASB changed from colorless to yellow in the presence of Al3+, which was attributed to the formation of the BASB·Al3+ complex. Fig. 13(d) showed that with the continuous addition of aluminum ions, the absorption spectrum of the receptor solution decreased at 373 nm and a new absorption peak appeared at 425 nm.

Cadmium is an important metal element, which is widely used in metallurgy, electroplating, chemical fertilizer, military and other industrial and agricultural fields. Therefore, it is easy to flow into the environment, causing serious harm to the environment and human beings. It can cause damage to human bones, kidneys and nervous system, and even cause cancer. In order to detect cadmium ions, Ye reported a proportional fluorescence probe based on 6-substituted quinoline Zn2+ complex (HQZn) [69]. Fig. 14 (a) showed a schematic diagram of the interaction between HQZn and Cd2+. The fluorescent color changed from green to yellow. With the constant addition of Cd2+ to methanol solution of HQZn, the fluorescence spectrum was redshifted, and the intensity decreased at 485 nm and increased at 560 nm, as shown in Fig. 14(b). The reaction principle was that Zn2+ in HQZn was replaced by Cd2+. Li reported a fluorescence sensor for embedding triethylene glycol triazole bonded silica nanospheres into CdSe quantum dots (CdSe@SiO2-TGT) [70]. The added sulfite anions interacted with the amino group on CDSE@siO2-TGT in electrons, which caused the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process that ultimately leaded to fluorescence quenching. The addition of Cd2+ then inhibited the PET process, resulting in the recovery and significant enhancement of the fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 14(c). After adding different metal ions to the solution of the sensor, only Cd2+ significantly enhanced the fluorescence of the solution, as shown in Fig. 14(d).

In addition to the fluorescent method, the colorimetric method can detect metal ions more quickly and sensitively. Zhang reported a newly synthesized graphite-like nitride-doped carbon quantum dot coated gold nanoparticles (Au@g-CNQDs) and used for colorimetric detection of Cd2+ [71]. The addition of Cd2+ caused the aggregation of Au@g-CNQDs and changed the color of the solution from wine red to blue-purple, as shown in Fig. 15 (a). After adding different concentrations of Cd2+ to the Au@g-CNQDs solution, the ratio of absorbance at 650 nm to that at 520 nm gradually increased, and the solution showed corresponding color changes, as shown in Fig. 15(b). Liu reported a porphyrin functionalized cotton fiber (CPT), which successfully detected and adsorbed cadmium ions [72]. Fig. 15(c) showed the preparation process of CPT and its reaction principle with Cd2+. Cotton fiber was selected as adsorbent substrate, and 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methy-4-pyridinio)porphyrin tetra (p-toluenesulfonate) (TMPyP) was selected as the color indicator of cadmium ions and fixed on the surface of cotton fiber. The color change of CPT after adsorption of cadmium ions was caused by charge transfer from metal to ligand.A cadmium ion bound to four nitrogen atoms on the TMPyP porphyrin ring, causing the plane structure of the TMPyP molecule to deviate. It can be seen from Fig. 15(d) that the absorbance of CPT sample increased linearly and the color changed from brown to dark green after adsorbing different initial concentrations of Cd2+.

In addition to fluorescence and colorimetric methods, Cd2+ can be effectively detected by absorption spectra and surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectra. Yin reported a new type of sensor based on surface-enhanced Raman scattering ((SERS)), in which the SERS-active nanoparticles were composed of gold nanoparticles and a layer of Cd2+ chelating polymer brush coating [73]. The addition of Cd2+ resulted in the aggregation of particles, which turned on SERS signal, as shown in Fig. 16 (a). Fig. 16(b) showed the change of surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectra after adding different metal ions, in which the addition of cadmium ions significantly enhanced the intensity of SERS. Yin reported a nanocomposite material MoS2@TMPyP, consisting of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanosheets and 5, 10, 15, 20-Tetrakis (1-methyl-4-pyridinio) porphyrintetra(p-toluenesulfonate) (TMPyP), for the detection and removal of Cd2+ [74]. The complexation rate of Cd2+ with MOS2@TMPyP was higher than that of Cd2+ and TMPyP. MoS2 increased the chance of contact between Cd2+ and TMPyP, thus accelerating their complexation, as shown in Fig. 16(c). With the continuous addition of Cd2+, the absorption spectrum of MoS2@TMPyP changed uniquely, as shown in Fig. 16(d).

Cobalt is a kind of trace element which has certain benign biological effects on human body and microorganisms. It is essential in the process of metabolism, red blood cell production and protein synthesis. However, excessive cobalt can cause serious harm to the human body, such as diarrhea, anemia, heart disease, lung disease and vasodilation and other health problems. Some industrial production processes caused cobalt pollution in the environment and threaten human health, so it is necessary to detect cobalt in the environment quickly and effectively. Mohamed reported a water-soluble CdS quantum dot (MPA-CdS QDS) modified with mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) as stabilizer, and Co2+ was detected successfully by fluorescence quenching mechanism [75]. Fig. 17 (a) showed the process of complex formation between MPA-CdS quantum dots and Co2+ through electrostatic interaction. The addition of Co2+ to QDs solution caused a sharp quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the whole solution, and the intensity gradually decreased with the concentration of Co2+ increasing, as shown in Fig. 17(b). The reason of quenching was the formation of MPA-CO2 + complex. Zeng reported a novel polycrystalline rare earth complex prepared by acetylacetone (ACAC) tuning method as a sensor for detecting Co2+ [76]. The addition of Co2+ had a significant quenching effect on the luminescence of the sensor, which was attributed to the unique external electron configuration of Co2+ that induced the dissociation of the polycrystalline rare earth complexes, as shown in Fig. 17(c). As shown in Fig. 17(d), in the presence of different metal ions (representing blank, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Na+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Cr3+ and Co2+ respectively from inside to the front), only Co2+ had obvious quenching effect on the sensor.

Compared with fluorescence method, colorimetric method can detect cobalt ions visually and quickly without complicated instruments. Zhang reported an etching method of gold nanorods (GNRs) based on Fenton-like reaction and applied it to colorimetric detection of cobalt ions [77]. Due to the Fenton-like reaction initiated by Co2+, H2O2 was decomposed to produce a large amount of superoxide anion free radicals (O2 .-), which caused GNRs to be oxidized, resulting in a color change from green to red, as shown in Fig. 18 (a). With the concentration of cobalt ions increasing, the ultraviolet extinction spectral peak of GNRs decreased and moved towards the shortwave direction, as shown in Fig. 18(b). Maity reported a novel colorimetric sensor for Co2+ based on coumarin coupled thiocarbanohydrazone [78]. The sensor reacted with Co2+ to form a complex, resulting in a significant color change from yellow to dark pink, as shown in Fig. 18(c). With the addition of Co2+, the absorption band of the sensor red-shifted from 470 nm to 510 nm, and the color of the solution changed from yellow to dark pink, as shown in Fig. 18(d).

Apart from the methods above, cobalt ions can also be detected by changes such as ultraviolet absorption spectra. Shaabani reported a bis (azophenol) calixarene derivative and studied its optical response to various metal ions [79]. It was found that there was a significant change in the absorption spectrum after combining with Co2+. Fig. 19 (a) showed that calixarene derivatives combined with Co2+ at a ratio of 1:1 to form the complex. When Co2+ was added to the DMSO solution of the calixarene, the UV–vis absorption band at 518 nm and 317 nm increased with the concentration of Co2+ increasing, as shown in Fig. 19(b). Sharma reported a new synthetic imine-connected receptor-coated ZnO sensor with organic receptor (containing imine bond) as the capping agent for the recognition of Co2+ [80]. Fig. 19(c) showed a diagram of imine receptor modified ZnO crystals, which can be used to detect nano-moles of Co2+. This was due to the imine attachment of the sensor which provided additional binding sites for the recognition of metal ions. When salt solution of Co2+ was gradually added to the HEPES buffered DMSO/H2O (8:2, v/v) containing the sensor, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was quenched at 445 nm and increased at 353 nm. And the ratio of fluorescence intensity at these two points was linearly related to the concentration of cobalt ion, as shown in Fig. 19(d).

Trivalent chromium (Cr3+) is one of the essential trace elements for human and other mammals, which plays an important role in the metabolism of fat, nucleic acid and protein in vivo. Chromium is also widely used in electroplating, metal processing, leather production and other industrial and agricultural fields, so it is easy to cause chromium pollution in the environment. Lack of chromium can lead to cardiovascular disease, diabetes and even neurological diseases. Therefore, it is necessary to detect Cr3+ in the environment. Liu reported a novel fluorescent probe based on rhodamine-urea methacrylate, which can be used for sensitive detection of Cr3+ [81]. Due to the molecular recognition of the urea part of the probe by Cr3+ and the accompanied spirocycle-open reaction, the combination of Cr3+ with the probe resulted in significant fluorescence enhancement, as shown in Fig. 20 (a). In the CH3CN-H2O (90/10, v/v, pH 7.0) solution containing the probe, the fluorescence intensity of the probe at 580 nm increased significantly with the increase of the concentration of Cr3+ (0–100 equivalent), as shown in Fig. 20(b). Sung reported a fluorescent probe based on the water-soluble CdSe quantum dots immobilized on the tip of an optical fiber in a sol–gel matrix for the detection of Cr3+ [82]. In the presence of Cr3+, the electron transfer reaction on the surface trap of CdSe QDs was formed rapidly, resulting in fluorescence quenching, which may be related to the paramagnetism of Cr3+, as shown in Fig. 20(c). In the solution of CdSe QDs, the effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of QDs was studied. It was found that only Cr3+ could significantly quench the fluorescence intensity of QDs, as shown in Fig. 20(d).

The colorimetric sensor based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) has high sensitivity to Cr3+. Shellaiah reported a sensor based on bare gold nanoparticles prepared by borohydride reduction method, which can effectively detect Cr3+ [83]. As can be seen from Fig. 21 (a), the addition of Cr3+ caused the aggregation of AuNPs, and the color of the solution changed rapidly from red to blue. Fig. 21(b) showed the color changes of AuNPs toward 0–2.5 μM of Cr3+ with respect to time(0–60 min). In the aqueous solution of AuNPs, with the increase of Cr3+ concentration, the absorption peak of AuNPs at 520 nm decreased gradually, while a new absorption peak appeared at 654 nm, and its intensity increased gradually, as shown in Fig. 21(c). With the increase of Cr3+ concentration (0.3–3 μm), the absorbance ratio (A654/A520) of AuNPs at 654 nm and 520 nm presented a linear relationship with the concentration of Cr3+, and the limit of detection (LOD) for Cr3+ was 13.42 nM, as shown in Fig. 21(D).

Copper is one of the indispensable trace elements in the human body, and it plays an important role in many physiological processes in the human body, such as serving as a catalytic factor of various enzymes, participating in metabolism and so on. Excessive copper in organisms can cause anemia, liver disease, Alzheimer's disease, Menkes syndrome and other serious diseases. Due to the wide application of copper in industrial and agricultural fields, such as alloy manufacturing, battery wire, chemical fertilizer manufacturing and so on, it is easy to cause pollution, which requires a rapid and efficient method for the detection of copper ions. Tian reported a fluorescence sensor based on ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets [84]. The sensing mechanism is that the redox potential of Cu2+ is between the valence band (VB) and the conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4. After the addition of Cu2+, it occurred the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect with the conduction band, thus quenching the fluorescence of the sensor, as shown in Fig. 22 (a). With the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of g-C3N4 nanosheets decreased significantly, as shown in Fig. 22(b). Suganya reported a new type of fluorescence sensor based on thiophene Schiff base, which detected copper ions in aqueous solution by fluorescence enhancement effect [85]. As shown in Fig. 22(c), the binding of Schiff base receptor to Cu2+ inhibited the original photoinduced electron transfer mechanism and enabled fluorescence to be turned on. With the continuous addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence of the receptor at 430 nm gradually increased, as shown in Fig. 22(d).

Colorimetric method also provides a convenient and efficient way to detect Cu2+. Wang reported a rhodamine-based fluorescent chemical sensor synthesized by the condensation reaction of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural and rhodamine B hydrazide, which recognized Cu2+ by obvious colorimetric phenomenon [86]. As shown in Fig. 23 (a), the color of the solution changed from colorless to pink after the sensor was combined with Cu2+, which was attributed to the addition of Cu2+ inducing the ring opening of rhodamine spiro lactam. By adding Cu2+ to the Tris-HCl buffer solution of the sensor and observing the change of the absorption spectrum, it can be seen that the intensity of the absorption spectrum increased linearly with the increase of the Cu2+ concentration (0 ~ 5 μ M), as shown in Fig. 23(b). Ye reported a successfully developed colorimetric probe based on rhodamine B and 5-ferrocenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole [87]. The probe reacted with Cu2+ to form a complex with a stoichiometric ratio of 1:1, as shown in Fig. 23(c). By studying the absorption response of the probe to various metal ions in EtOH /HEPES(8:2, V/V, pH7.2) solution, it was found that only the addition of Cu2+ caused a color transition from colorless to pink, and a strong absorption band centered at 554 nm was formed in the absorption spectrum, which was attributed to the the ring opening of rhodamine spironolactam induced by Cu2+. As the concentration of Cu2+ increased, the absorbance at 554 nm gradually increased, as shown in Fig. 23(d).

In addition to the above methods, Cu2+ can also be effectively detected by specific changes in absorption spectrum or fluorescence spectrum. Zong reported a probe based on fluorescent silica nanoparticles, which can be used for rapid and effective detection of Cu2+ [88]. Fig. 24 (a) clearly showed the binding mode of the probe and Cu2+. Polyethylenimine (PEI) was covalently grafted onto the surface of dye-doped silica nanoparticles by hyperbranched surface polymerization, and then the fluorescence dye rhodamine B isothiocyanate (RBITC) was covalently connected through the reaction of primary amine groups on PEI with thiocyanate on RBITC molecules. Moreover, PEI as a strong complexing agent of Cu2+ can selectively detect Cu2+.With the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe at 580 nm gradually decreased, as shown in Fig. 24(b). Hu reported a sensor based on hydrazine, which was successfully applied to the colorimetric detection of Cu2+ [89]. Fig. 24(c) expressly showed the coordination mode and deprotonation mechanism between the sensor and Cu2+. The possible mechanism was Cu2+ induced deprotonation of the receptor. After adding different concentrations of Cu2+ to the THF/water(9:1, v/v) containing HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.0), with the concentration of Cu2+ increasing, the intensity of the absorption spectrum at 336 nm gradually decreased, while a new absorption band appeared at 502 nm and gradually enhanced, as shown in Fig. 24(d).

Iron is the most abundant transition metal element in organisms. Fe2+ ions and Fe3+ ions play an important role in many physiological processes, such as the transport and storage of oxygen, metabolism and so on. The imbalance of iron concentration can lead to serious diseases such as anemia, hepatitis, Parkinson's disease and nervous system damage. Iron also plays an important role in industry, medicine and biology, so it is necessary to develop convenient and effective methods for the detection of iron ions. Wu reported a new glutathione modified CdTe quantum dot (GSH-CdTe QDs) which can detect Fe2+ by fluorescence quenching effect [90]. Fe2+ reacted with H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals with oxidizing nature, which made the electrons transfer from the traps of quantum dots to hydroxyl radicals to induce fluorescence quenching, as shown in Fig. 25 (a). As can be seen from Fig. 25(b), with the continuous addition of Fe2+, the fluorescence intensity of QDs significantly decreased. Based on the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer, Kumar successfully connected rhodamine with dimethylaminocinnamaldehyde to form a new fluorescence sensor [91]. Fe2+ bound to the rhodamine spirolactam ring of the sensor and opened it to amide form, thus enhancing the intramolecular FRET, as shown in Fig. 25(c). With the concentration of Fe2+ increasing, the fluorescent intensity of the sensor decreased gradually at 470 nm, while a new peak was generated at 582 nm and enhanced continuously, as shown in Fig. 25(d).

Compared with fluorescent method, colorimetric method is also an effective and rapid method for the detection of Fe2+. Ondigo reported a novel colorimetric probe for the detection of Fe2+, which was synthesized by functionalized poly (vinylbenzyl chloride) (PVBC) nanofibers using 2-(2′-pyridine) imidazole (PIMH) as the ligand [92]. Fig. 26 (a) showed the binding mode of the probe and Fe2+, accompanied by a significant color shift from yellow to red. Fig. 26(b) studied the UV absorption spectrum of the mixed solution of the probe and Fe2+. With the increase of Fe2+ concentration, the light absorption intensity at 484 nm increased gradually, as shown in Figure (b). This was due to the spin crossover (SCO) effect from high spin Fe2+ to low spin Fe2+ when Fe2+ interacted with PIMH. Zhang reported a new sensor based on phenylene–acetylene, which can detect Fe2 + through obvious colorimetric phenomena [93]. Fig. 26(c) showed the bonding modes of the sensor with Fe2+, Cd2+ and Zn2+, respectively. Among them, it recognized Fe2+ by colorimetric phenomenon and detected Cd2+ and Zn2+ by different fluorescence colors. After adding Fe2+ to the EtOH-H2O (1/1, v/v) solution of the sensor, the absorption spectrum of the solution produced a new absorption peak at 573 nm accompanied by a significant color change from colorless to fuchsia. This was due to the N-metal coordination effect caused by the addition of Fe2+. Moreover, as the concentration of Fe2+ gradually increased, the light absorption intensity at 573 nm increased linearly, as shown in Fig. 26(d). This was attributed to the N-metal coordination effect.

In addition to the fluorescence effect and colorimetric change, the change of phosphorescence spectrum can also effectively detect Fe2+. Jin reported a method for detecting Fe2+ based on phosphor 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) coated with manganese doped ZnS quantum dots (MP-Mn: ZnS quantum dots)-Fenton hybrid system [94]. Due to the hydroxyl radical produced by the Fenton reaction of Fe2+ with H2O2, the phosphorescence of MPA-Mn: ZnS QDs was quenched, as shown in Fig. 27 (a). With the continuous addition of Fe2+, the phosphorescence of MPA-Mn: ZnS QDs was quenched, and the intensity decreased gradually, as shown in Fig. 27(b). Fig. 27(c) showed that the phosphor intensity of quantum dots linearly increased with the concentration of Fe2+ increasing.

Iron plays many important roles in the body, such as oxygen metabolism, DNA synthesis, enzymatic assistance and so on. Lack or excess of iron can lead to serious diseases such as anemia, low immune function, organ damage, and cancer. Moreover, the trivalent form of iron is indispensable to living system. Its deficiency and excess can cause neurological diseases, diabetes, hepatitis and various cancers. In view of the importance of iron, it is necessary to develop efficient, rapid and sensitive detection methods to detect iron ions in the environment and cells. Ananthanarayanan reported a high-efficiency green electrochemical method that can obtain BMIM+ -functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from 3D graphene, which can detect Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching mechanism [95]. In this method, GQDs were obtained by electrochemically incising 3D graphene using room temperature ionic liquid (1-butyl-3-methylimidazoliumhexafl-uorophosphate (BMIMPF6) in acetonitrile) as the electrolyte, and then Fe3+ was added to make GQDs accumulate, which caused fluorescence quenching, as shown in Fig. 28 (a). With the concentration of Fe3+ increasing, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched gradually, as shown in Fig. 28(b). Sui reported a novel fluorescence sensor synthesized by using boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) as a fluorophore and 1, 10-diaz-18-corown-6 based cryptand as the recognition element of Fe3+, which detected Fe3 + through fluorescence enhancement mechanism [96]. After the combination of the sensor and Fe3+, the PET process from the cryptand to the BODIPY fluorophore was suppressed, thus the fluorescence emission was significantly enhanced, as shown in Fig. 28(c). When 1–700 equivalent of Fe3+ was continuously added to the H2O-CH3CN (9:1 v/v) solution of the sensor, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor gradually increased, and within the range of 0–200 equivalent, the emission intensity was linearly related to the concentration of Fe3+. After that, the strength increased slowly and finally tended to be stable, as shown in Fig. 28(d).

Compared with the fluorescence method, the colorimetric method is more direct and effective, and the cost is lower. Narayanaswamy reported a novel aldazine ligand (8-hydroxyjulolidinal-azine (JA)) synthesized by simple condensation reaction of 8-hydroxyjulolidinal and hydrazine, which can be used as a colorimetric sensor for detection of Fe3+ [97]. After JA was combined with Fe3+, the color changed from light yellow to purple, as shown in Fig. 29 (a). The principle of recognition was based on the twisted plane intramolecular charge transfer. Fe3+ was continuously added to the acetonitrile solution of the ligand JA, and the absorption band of JA at 445 nm gradually red-shifted to 575 nm, as shown in Fig. 29(b). Wang reported a novel fluorescent probe based on the reaction of ethylenediamine with N-stabilization rhodamine [98]. The addition of Fe3+ caused an obvious color change from colorless to red, which was attributed to the opening of spirolactam ring caused by the combination of Fe3+ and probe, as shown in Fig. 29(c). In the aqueous solution of the probe, the absorbance of the probe at 562 nm was significantly enhanced with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 29(d).

In addition to the above methods, some specific spectral changes can be used to detect Fe3+ sensitively and efficiently. Saleem reported a novel Schiff base derivative synthesized by the reaction of 4-amino-3-(2-fluorobenzyl)-1H-1,2,4-triazole-5 (4H)-thione 5 with thiophene-2-carbaldehyde under microwave irradiation as a highly efficient fluorescence probe for the detection of Fe3+ [99]. Fig. 30 (a) showed the chelating mechanism and reversibility of the probe with Fe3+ in the presence of excess potassium cyanide. By titrating the solution of the probe with Fe3+, it was found that the continuous addition of Fe3+ caused the decrease of the absorption intensity at 253 nm and 293 nm, and a new absorption signal appeared at 357 nm. With the increase of the concentration of Fe3+, the absorption intensity gradually increased, as shown in Fig. 30(b). As the concentration of Fe3+ increased, the fluorescence intensity of the probe at 478 nm gradually quenched, as shown in Fig. 30(c). These phenomena were attributed to the conformational change of the structure caused by the combination of Fe3+ and probe.

Mercury is one of the most toxic heavy metal elements, which is seriously harmful to the environment and human health. Mercury pollution mainly comes from chemical manufacturing, coal and other fossil fuel combustion and volcanic eruption. Mercury poisoning can cause damage to the brain, kidney, nervous system and other organs, leading to serious diseases such as cardiomyopathy and cancer. Therefore, it is necessary to develop methods that can effectively detect mercury ions in water and food. Fan reported a new chemical sensor (SQ-1) based on squaraine dye, which can detect and identify Hg2+ through fluorescence quenching mechanism [100]. Hg2+ complexed with SQ-1, which destroyed the donor–acceptor-donor (D-A-D) charge transfer and caused the quenching of the fluorescence signal, as shown in Fig. 31 (a). When Hg2+ (0–110 μ M) was gradually added to the ethanol aqueous solution of SQ-1, the fluorescence intensity of SQ-1 was gradually quenched with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, as shown in Fig. 31(b). Wanichacheva reported a novel fluorescent chemical sensor based on the connection of fluorescein to two rhodamine B fluorophores via hydrazide moieties, and successfully realized the effective detection of Hg2+ [101]. The binding of the sensor and Hg2+ led to the formation of spirolactam ring-opening complexes of fluorescein and rhodamine B, and the fluorescence signal of the sensor at the 575 nm was enhanced by the FRET process from fluorescein to rhodamine B, as shown in Fig. 31(c). In the dichloromethane solution of the sensor, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor at 575 nm was significantly enhanced with the concentration of Hg2+ increasing, as shown in Fig. 31(d). Huang reported a time-gated fluorescence resonance energy transfer (TGFRET) sensing technique based on manganese-doped quantum dots and gold nanoparticles [102]. The quantum dots and gold nanoparticles were functionalized by two single-stranded DNA respectively. When Hg2+ was added to the solution containing quantum dots and gold nanoparticles, it combined with two thymine (T) bases to form stable T-Hg2+-T base pairs, which initiated the DNA hybridization. As a result, the energy of the quantum dots was transferred to the gold nanoparticles, and the fluorescence intensity of the solution was significantly reduced, as shown in Fig. 31(e). In the solution of quantum dots and gold nanoparticles, the time-gated fluorescence emission intensity of the solution decreased with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, and this change can be observed at very low mercury ion concentration, as shown in Fig. 31(f).

Compared with the fluorescence method, the colorimetric method has the advantages of simple operation and low cost. Nidya reported a novel cysteine-modified silver nanoparticles probe for colorimetric detection of Hg2+ [103]. As shown in Fig. 32 (a), the cysteine-modified silver nanoparticle probe complexed with Hg2+ in a specific way to form a silver amalgam, while other metal ions did not. With the continuous addition of Hg2+, the color of cys-Ag sol gradually changed from honey brown to transparent, and the absorption peak was quenched, as shown in Fig. 32(b). However, other metal ions did not cause obvious color changes. Farhadi reported a colorimetric sensor based on biologically green synthesized silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) for the detection of Hg2+ [104]. As shown in Fig. 32(c), redox reaction was occurred between the added Hg2+ and Ag NPs, which gradually changed the color of solution from yellowish brown to colorless. In the solution of Ag NPs, the absorption peak of Ag NPs at 408 nm decreased with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, as shown in Fig. 32(d).

Hg2+ can also be effectively detected by specific changes in the circular dichroism or absorption spectrum. Nan reported a new type of noble metal–organic hybrid chiral complex nanoparticles with high optical activity, named chiral Ag-l-cysteine complex nanoparticles, which can be used as probes for the detection of Hg2+ [105]. After Hg2+ replaced the Ag+ in chiral Ag-l-cysteine complex nanoparticles through displacement reaction, the achiral structure was formed, resulting in the change of circular dichroism (CD), as shown in Fig. 33 (a). With the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the intensity of CD spectrum of probe colloid at 254 nm decreased gradually, as shown in Fig. 33(b). Li reported a new method for the detection of Hg2+ by nucleic acid-treated AuNPs mixtures [106]. In the stable mixture of AuNPs and nucleic acid, Hg2+ was combined with nucleic acid to form a hairpin complex, and AuNPs aggregation was induced, as shown in Fig. 33(c). The curve (a)-(f) in Fig. 33(d) corresponded to the absorption spectrum of nucleic acid/AuNPs mixture in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (a-f: 0–5 × 10-6M). With the concentration of Hg2+ increasing, the corresponding redshift band increased gradually.

Pb2+ is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions. Lead poisoning can cause serious harm to human brain, nervous system, kidney and other organs, resulting in muscle paralysis, neurosis, brain edema and other diseases. In particular, it does great harm to the brain development of children. Lead pollution can be caused by paint, petroleum industry and alloy manufacturing, so it is necessary to develop a rapid and effective method for the detection of Pb2+. Zhang reported a novel fluorescence sensor which was constructed using C-PS2.M−DNA−templated silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) [107]. The DNA template was composed of two segments: one was the Ag-NCs nucleation segment located at both ends of the DNA template, and the other was the aptamer segment of the Pb2+ located in the middle of the DNA template. The addition of Pb2+ induced the conformational change of Pb2+ aptamer in the template, which made DNA-Ag NCs bind tightly, thus greatly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs, as shown in Fig. 34 (a). With the increase of Pb2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of C-PS2.M−DNA−Ag NCs gradually increased, as shown in Fig. 34(b). Yao reported a sensitive and effective method to detect Pb2+ using the fluorescence quenching characteristics of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) and its ability to bind single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) [108]. As shown in Fig. 34(c), the substrate (blue) labeled by Cy3 was hybridized with 8-17DNAzyme (green) to generate dsDNA, which was not easy to be adsorbed on the surface of SWCNT, thus presenting high fluorescence. Then the addition of Pb2+ catalyzed DNAzyme to divide the fluorescence-labeled substrate into two halves, and the released single-chain products were adsorbed on SWCNT, resulting in complete fluorescence quenching. With the increase of Pb2+ concentration (1 nm ~ 10 μm), the pyrolysis yield of substrate increased and fluorescence intensity decreased, as shown in Fig. 34(d).

Colorimetric method can detect Pb2+ quickly and effectively with the advantages of low cost and simple operation. Chai reported a colorimetric probe based on glutathione functionalized gold nanoparticles (GSH-GNPs) for rapid and sensitive detection of Pb2+ [109]. The Pb2+ in aqueous solution was captured by glutathione, resulting in the aggregation of GNPs, which changed the color of solution from red to blue, as shown in Fig. 35 (a). With the increase of Pb2+ concentration, the absorption spectrum of GSH-GNPS gradually redshifted, as shown in Fig. 35(b). The illustration in Fig. 35(b) showed a linear relationship between the absorbance ratio (A700/520) and the concentration of Pb2+. Wang designed and reported a novel Pb2+ sensor based on polydiacetylene (PDA). The main part of the sensor was a novel conjugated polymer (Poly-PDA-L) synthesized by derivation of the monomers 1-(1,4,7,10,13-pentaoxa-16-aza-cyclooctadec-16-yl)-pentacosa-10,12-diynamide (PCDA-L) and 10,12-pentacosadiynoic acid (PCDA) [110]. The combination of the head group (1-aza-18-crown-6-ether) of PCDA-L with Pb2+ released the strain energy applied on the alkyl side chains produced during the polymerization, resulting in changes in the color and optical properties of the solution, as shown in Fig. 35(c). In the HEPES buffer of poly-PDA-L (10 mM,pH = 7.4), with the increase of Pb2+ concentration, the color of the solution changed from blue to purple, while the absorption intensity at 640 nm decreased gradually and the absorption intensity at 540 nm increased gradually, as shown in Fig. 35(d).

In addition to common fluorescence methods and colorimetric methods, Pb2+ can also be effectively and sensitively detected by changes in surface enhanced Raman scattering and phosphorescence spectra. Chen reported a novel room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) sensor based on glutathione-capped manganese doped ZnS quantum dots (GSH-Mn-ZnS), which can be used for simple and effective detection of Pb2+ [111]. The addition of Pb2+ effectively quenched the room temperature phosphorescence of GSH-Mn-ZnS QDs, which was attributed to the strong binding of Pb2+ to the surface of glutathione coating, as shown in Fig. 36 (a). The RTP intensity of QDs was proportionally quenched with the increase of Pb2+ concentration, in the Tris-HCl buffer containing GSH-Mn-ZnS QDs, as shown in Fig. 36(b). Frost designed and reported a novel sensor for the detection of Pb2+ based on surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) of citrate functionalized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [112]. Due to the high affinity of citric acid to heavy metals, citrate molecules interacted with Pb2+ through carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which caused the aggregation of AuNPs, thus changing its surface enhanced Raman spectra, as shown in Fig. 36(c). As the concentration of Pb2+ increased, the intensity of each band in SERS spectrum changed specifically, as shown in Fig. 36(d).

Palladium is an important excessive metal, which plays an important role in experiments and industrial fields, such as petroleum fuel industry, automobile industry, chemical products manufacturing, jewelry manufacturing and so on. As a result, a lot of palladium pollutants have been released into the environment. Pd2+ can compound with biological macromolecules such as DNA and protein, resulting in DNA degradation, allergic reactions and cytotoxicity and other serious harm. Therefore, it is particularly important to study and design an efficient and sensitive method for the detection of Pd2 + . Tang reported a novel fluorescent probe based on the newly developed 2-(2-hydroxyphenyl) quinazoline-4 (3H)-one derived enaminone for the detection of Pd2+ [113]. The probe was combined with Pd2+ at a stoichiometric ratio of 1:1, and the fluorescence of the probe was quenched due to the paramagnetism of Pd2+, as shown in Fig. 37 (a). In the PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) of the probe, the fluorescence intensity of the solution at 538 nm decreased with the increase of the concentration of Pd2+ (0–1.5 equiv.), as shown in Fig. 37(b). Chen reported a novel fluorescent probe synthesized by 1H-benzimidazole-2-ethanamine and a rhodamine B moiety [114]. After the probe was combined with Pd2+, the spiro lactam ring of rhodamine was opened into the acyclic xanthene form and generated strong fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 37(c). The fluorescence intensity of the probe at 569 nm increased linearly with the gradual increase of Pd2+ concentration (0–60 μM), in EtOH/H2O (1:1, v/v) containing the probe, as shown in Fig. 37(d). Liu reported a novel fluorescent probe based on surfactant micelle (Triton X-100) coated with 2-phenyl-3-(prop2-ynyloxy)-4H-chromen-4-one (PPC), which can efficiently detect Pd2+ [115]. Under the action of Pd2+, PPC was degraded to 3-hydroxy-2-phenyl-4 h-chromen-4-one (HPC), and the fluorescence was enhanced. However, water molecules in pure water quenched this fluorescence, while hydrophobic nuclei in Triton X-100 micelles prevented the influence of water molecules on fluorescence, thus enhancing fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 37(e). In pure water containing the probe, the fluorescence intensity of the probe at 526 nm gradually increased with the concentration of Pd2+ (0–60 μM) increasing, as shown in Fig. 37(f).

Compared with fluorescence method, colorimetric method can also identify Pd2+ effectively. Yang reported a novel colorimetric sensor based on rhodamine [116]. The combination of the sensor and Pd2+ induced the ring-opening of rhodamine spiro lactam, resulting in significant color change and fluorescence enhancement, as shown in Fig. 38 (a). In the methanol/PBS (1:1, v/v, pH 7.4) solution of the sensor, only Pd2 + caused a significant color change after different metal ions were added. And the absorption intensity of the sensor at 555 nm increased significantly with the increase of Pd2+ concentration, as shown in Fig. 38(b). Li designed and reported a new rhodamine-based propargyl ether derivative (L), as a sensor for the detection of Pd2+ [117]. After the complexation of L and Pd2+, the color of the solution changed from yellow to pink, which was attributed to the depropargylation of the hydroxyl groups mediated by Pd2+, as shown in Fig. 38(c). In the CH3CN-H2O containing L, with the increase of Pd2+ concentration, the absorption intensity of L at 394 nm gradually shifted to 432 nm, and a new absorption peak appeared at 558 nm, which was gradually enhanced, as shown in Fig. 38(d).

In addition, when some sensors and probes detect Pd2+, there are fluorescence changes and colorimetric changes at the same time. Li reported a ratio fluorescent probe (RN3) based on 7-substituted coumarin and rhodamine B to detect Pd2+ by FRET mechanism [118]. As shown in Fig. 39 (a), RN3 emitted green fluorescence in the absence of Pd2+. And the addition of Pd2+ induced the ring opening of spiro lactam rhodamine B, which emitted red fluorescence due to FRET process. The absorption intensity of RN3 in the range of 530–600 nm gradually increased with the increase of Pd2+ concentration, accompanied by color change, in methanol–water solution (1:99, v/v, 50 mM, PBS buffer, pH = 7.4) containing RN3, as shown in Fig. 39(b). With the continuous addition of Pd2+, the intensity of RN3 at 515 nm gradually weakened, while a new peak appeared at 575 nm and increased continuously, in methanol–water solution (1:99, v/v, 50 mM, PBS buffer, pH = 7.4) containing RN3, as shown in Fig. 39(c).

Zinc is the second richest transition metal element in human body which plays a very important role in various biological processes of human body, such as enzyme catalysis, gene expression, nerve signal transmission and so on. The imbalance of Zn2+ in the body can lead to serious health problems such as Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, brain loss, epilepsy and cancer. Due to the extensive application of Zn in the medical field, automobile hardware electroplating and other industrial fields, zinc pollution in the natural environment has been caused. Therefore, it is urgent to design and develop efficient and sensitive sensor for the detection of Zn2+. Dong reported a new Schiff base oxime sensor (H2L) 1- (2-hydroxy-3 - (2-hydroxybenzylidene) amino) phenyl) ethenone methyl oxime based on condensation reaction, which has a high selectivity to Zn2+ [119]. H2L exhibited weak fluorescence due to its intramolecular photo-induced electron transfer (PET) effect, but when combined with Zn2+, the intramolecular PET effect was inhibited, resulting in greatly enhanced fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 40 (a). In the Tris-HCl buffer (MeOH/H2O = 9:1, v/v, pH 7.2) containing H2L, with the increase of Zn2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of H2L gradually increased and blue shifted by 14 nm, as shown in Fig. 40(b). Anand reported a novel vitamin B6 cofactor derivative 3-hydroxy-N'-((3 hydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)-2-methylpyridin-4-yl) methylene)-2-naphthohydrazide (NPY) synthesized by one-pot condensation reaction of pyridoxal with 3-hydroxy-2-naphthoichydrazide, as a fluorescence probe for the detection of Zn2+ [120]. As shown in Fig. 40(c), NPY combined with Zn2+ through the imine-N, pyridoxal-OH and carbonyl-O of naphthyl units to form a complex, which caused the change of fluorescence color. With the concentration of Zn2+ increasing, the emission intensity of NPY at 530 nm decreased gradually, and a new blue shift band appeared at 475 nm with gradually increased intensity, as shown in Fig. 40(d).

In addition to fluorescence method, colorimetry can recognize Zn2+ by intuitive color changes. Vidya reported a fluorescein dye containing imidazole synthesized by the condensation of imidazole carboxaldehyde with fluorescein hydrazide in methanol, which was used as a colorimetric probe for the detection of Zn2+ [121]. The combination of the probe and Zn2+ transformed the spiro lactam ring into an open amide form, resulting in a significant color change from colorless to greenish yellow, as shown in Fig. 41 (a). In the aqueous DMSO (DMSO/H2O = 1:9) containing the probe, after adding various metal ions, only the addition of Zn2+ made the probe produce a new absorption band at 505 nm, and the absorption band at 335 nm had a small red shift, as shown in Fig. 41(b). Lee reported a new type of chemical sensor (Rh6G-Cin) based on rhodamine, which can effectively detect Zn2+ [122]. Fig. 41(c) showed the synthesis route of the sensor Rh6G-Cin. It combined with Zn2+ to form a complex [Rh6G-Cin-Zn2+], resulting in the color of solution changing from colorless to pink, which was attributed to the ring opening of rhodamine spiro lactam induced by the addition of Zn2+. In the CH3CN solution of Rh6G-Cin, with the increase of Zn2+ concentration, a new absorption band was appeared in the absorption spectrum of Rh6G-Cin at 557 nm, and its intensity gradually increased, as shown in Fig. 41(d).

Moreover, some special sensors can also realize the effective detection of Zn2+. Peng reported a novel fluorescent probe synthesized by assembling lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) with chromophores [123]. As shown in Fig. 42 (a), NaYF4: Yb/Tm@NaYF4 with blue upconversion luminescence (UCL) was selected as the upconversion nanocrystals. After UCNPs was modified with polyacrylic acid (PAA) and compound 1 (abbreviated as 1-PAA-UCNPs), the original UCL was quenched. In the presence of Zn2+, the UCL signal was restored due to the coordination between Zn2+ and compound 1. In the presence of Zn2+, the absorbance of 1-PAA-UCNPs was significantly blue shifted from 475 nm to 360 nm due to the coordination of Zn2+ with compound 1, resulting in the recovery of UCL signal, as shown in Fig. 42(b). In the solution of 1-PAA-UCNPs (1 mg/mL), with the continuous addition of Zn2+, the UCL intensity at 475 nm gradually increased, as shown in Fig. 42(c).

The optical detection methods of Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Pd2+, Zn2+ were reviewed in detail here. The principles of optical detection were mainly based on photoinduced electron transfer, intramolecular charge transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, as well as the quantum dots and nanoparticles with specific optical properties, and the optical properties of rhodamine and its derivatives. The main optical detection methods for each ion were summarized. Different metal ions were selectively detected by fluorescence, colorimetric and other spectral changes.

The principle and methods of detection were summarized. The fluorescence sensor based on PET, ICT and FRET interacted with different metal ions to cause the fluorescence change of the whole system, which was specifically manifested as fluorescence quenching, fluorescence enhancement or specific change of fluorescence spectrum. The probe based on quantum dots and nanoparticles can selectively detect metal ions by interacting with metal ions and causing fluorescence or colorimetric changes by itself or surface complexes. The sensor based on rhodamine and its derivatives can detect metal ions by fluorescence, colorimetric and other spectral changes due to the optical properties of rhodamine. In fluorescence detection, with the change of ion concentration, the fluorescence intensity was gradually enhanced or quenched. In colorimetric detection, the system had significant color changes in the presence of different ion concentrations. In other spectral detection, the spectra showed specific changes with the increase of metal ion concentration.

Up to now, metal ion sensors based on optical methods had been used in many applications. Such as the detection of heavy metal ions in environment, water quality and food; Tracking and detection of metal ions in biological cells; Imaging and detection in the field of biomedicine, etc. There is no doubt that it has a good development prospect and its application scope will be more and more extensive. At the same time, it also faces the challenge of how to realize the detection of various metal ions more effectively, and how to realize the detection in cells and living bodies better. On the other hand, it will be the development direction in the future to study the mechanism of new detection methods and explore the metal ion sensors with excellent performance, environmental friendliness and reusable advantages.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiangfu Wang received his PhD degree from Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, in 2012. From 2012 to present, he works as an associate professor in the Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications, China. His current research concentrates on optoelectronic devices, and sensors and photoluminescence of rare-earth ions doped nano-materials and transparent glass ceramics.

Chengqian Shen obtained his Bachelor degree from Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications. From 2019 to present, he works in Xiangfu Wang’s group. His current research concentrates on optical detection of metal ions.

Chengfeng Zhou obtained his bachelor’s degree from College of Tongda, Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications in 2018. From 2018 to present, he has worked for his master’s degree in Xiangfu Wang’s group. His current research concentrates on the design of color-changing anti-counterfeit glass.

Yanyan Bu obtained her PhD degree from Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications in 2017. From 2017 to present, she works as senior engineer in Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications. Her current research concentrates on optical temperature sensing of rare-earth ions doped transparent glass ceramics.

Xiaohong Yan received his PhD degree from Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Science, in 1997. From 2010 to present, he works as a professor in the College of Electronic Science and Engineering at Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications. His current research concentrates on transport and manipulation of quantum system and new energy materials.
